platforms.name: Codeforces
platforms.desc: Competitive programming for speed and accuracy

interview_reviews.question: Find the minimum window substring containing all characters
interview_reviews.quote: 90-min OA on Google's platform. Two problems — one medium sliding window, one hard DP. Had to pass all test cases.

interview_reviews.question: Design and implement an LRU Cache
interview_reviews.quote: 45-min Google Doc coding. They wanted clean code with O(1) get/put. Asked about thread-safety as follow-up.

interview_reviews.question: Longest Increasing Subsequence
interview_reviews.quote: Expected O(n log n) patience sorting approach. Started with DP O(n²), then optimized. Communication was key.

interview_reviews.question: Number of Islands (BFS variation)
interview_reviews.quote: Graph traversal with a twist — had to handle diagonal connections and count distinct island shapes.

interview_reviews.question: Design Google Docs collaborative editing
interview_reviews.quote: Needed deep knowledge of OT/CRDT for conflict resolution. They valued breadth — asked about auth, storage, CDN too.

interview_reviews.question: Tell me about a time you disagreed with your team
interview_reviews.quote: STAR format expected. They value intellectual humility and low-ego collaboration stories. Be genuine.

insider_tips: Google codes in Google Docs — practice without IDE autocomplete
Always start with brute force, then optimize — they want to see your thinking process
Communication matters as much as correctness — explain while you code
DP and Graphs appear in 60%+ of coding rounds — master these first
For System Design, draw diagrams — use boxes, arrows, label everything
The 'Googliness' round is real — prepare 5-6 STAR stories about collaboration

do: Explain your thought process out loud
Write clean, readable code with proper variable names
Ask clarifying questions before diving in
dont: Jump to code without discussing approach
Use brute force without acknowledging better solutions exist
Skip edge cases — null inputs, empty arrays, overflow
Memorize solutions — interviewers can tell
Panic if stuck — ask for a hint, it's okay

impressive_projects.title: Serverless Data Pipeline
impressive_projects.desc: AWS Lambda + SQS/SNS event-driven pipeline processing millions of events with DLQ and monitoring.

impressive_projects.title: Inventory Management System
impressive_projects.desc: Multi-warehouse inventory with real-time stock updates, low-stock alerts, and demand forecasting.

must_have: Scalable backend with proper error handling
AWS services experience is a strong signal

behavioral.name: Day One Careers LP Blog
behavioral.desc: Official Amazon LP page — understand directly from the source

platforms.name: HackerRank
platforms.desc: Amazon OA is often on HackerRank

interview_reviews.question: Find the optimal delivery route (variation of Traveling Salesman)
interview_reviews.quote: 70-min OA with 2 coding + Work Simulation. The work sim tests LP alignment — Customer Obsession was key.

interview_reviews.question: Merge K Sorted Lists
interview_reviews.quote: Used min-heap approach. After coding, got 15 min of LP questions about 'Ownership' — had strong STAR stories ready.

interview_reviews.question: Design a LRU Cache with TTL
interview_reviews.quote: Standard LRU + expiry twist. LP focus was on 'Dive Deep' — they asked how I debugged a production issue.

interview_reviews.question: Behavioral deep-dive on Leadership Principles
interview_reviews.quote: Pure behavioral — 45 min of 'tell me about a time...' questions. They drill 3-4 follow-ups per story. Weak stories = reject.

interview_reviews.question: System Design: Design an order tracking system
interview_reviews.quote: Mix of LLD and project discussion. Focus on scale — Amazon processes millions of orders daily.

insider_tips: Leadership Principles are EVERYTHING — prepare 2 STAR stories per LP
The Bar Raiser can reject you even if all tech rounds pass — take behavioral seriously
Amazon OA work simulation is LP-based — always pick the 'Customer Obsession' answer
For coding, they want working code — not pseudocode. Test with edge cases
System Design for SDE-1 is lighter — focus on LLD and API design
'Bias for Action' is key — show you take initiative without being asked

do: Prepare 2 STAR stories for EACH Leadership Principle
Mention metrics and impact in behavioral answers
Think about scale — Amazon is massive
dont: Give generic behavioral answers without specific examples
Ignore the Work Simulation in OA — it's heavily weighted
Skip testing your code
Badmouth previous employers in LP stories
Forget to mention customer impact in your stories

impressive_projects.title: Content Recommendation System

impressive_projects.title: Graph-based Social Network
impressive_projects.desc: Friend suggestions using graph traversal, mutual friends algorithm, and degree-of-connection analysis.

Graph/network-related project is a huge plus

system_design.name: Meta Engineering Blog
system_design.desc: Official Meta engineering blog — understand how they build at scale

behavioral.name: Meta Culture Guide
behavioral.desc: Deep prep guide for Meta behavioral round

interview_reviews.question: Valid Parentheses with wildcards
interview_reviews.quote: 45-min CoderPad. Two medium-level problems. Speed matters — need to solve both. Used HashMap and greedy approach.

interview_reviews.question: Binary Tree Right Side View + Random Pick with Weight
interview_reviews.quote: BFS level-order problem followed by prefix-sum binary search. They push for optimal solutions — O(n) or bust.

interview_reviews.question: Design Instagram News Feed
interview_reviews.quote: Focus on fan-out-on-write vs fan-out-on-read. Discussed caching strategy, CDN for images, and ranking algorithm.

interview_reviews.question: Tell me about a time you had to make a difficult technical decision
interview_reviews.quote: Meta values 'Move Fast' and 'Be Bold'. Show you can make decisions with incomplete info and iterate.

insider_tips: Meta introduced AI-enabled coding rounds in 2025 — you can use AI assistance but must explain everything
Speed is critical — solve 2 problems in 45 min. Practice timed sessions
They love graph and tree problems — BFS/DFS variations appear very frequently
System Design focuses on Meta products — practice designing Feed, Messenger, Instagram
Behavioral round is shorter but important — 'Move Fast' and 'Be Bold' are key values
Clean code matters more than clever code — use clear variable names

do: Solve problems quickly — speed is tested
Use HashMaps and optimal data structures
Design systems around Meta's actual products for SD rounds
Show product sense — understand why features exist
dont: Take too long on brute force — jump to optimal faster
Ignore follow-up questions
Design systems without considering scale (billions of users)
Skip edge cases in coding
Be passive in behavioral — show ownership and initiative

behavioral.name: Growth Mindset Guide
behavioral.desc: Prepare for Microsoft's growth mindset culture

platforms.name: Codility
platforms.desc: Microsoft OA is often on Codility

interview_reviews.question: Two Sum variant + String manipulation
interview_reviews.quote: Codility 90-min OA, 3 problems. Medium difficulty but you need to solve 2/3 cleanly to advance.

interview_reviews.question: BST operations — find incorrectly placed nodes
interview_reviews.quote: Tree-heavy round. They wanted clean recursive code with proper null checks. Dry-run was mandatory.

interview_reviews.question: Modified Bipartite Graph problem
interview_reviews.quote: Graph coloring problem. Started with BFS approach, optimized with Union-Find. They valued the optimization journey.

interview_reviews.question: Mix of behavioral + project deep-dive + easy coding
interview_reviews.quote: 30 min project discussion, 15 min 'growth mindset' behavioral. They value learning from failures.

insider_tips: Microsoft values 'Growth Mindset' — show you love learning from mistakes
OA is on Codility — practice timed problems there specifically
Trees and Graphs are the most common topics — BST operations especially
They want executable code, not pseudocode — test your solution
The AA/HM round is lighter — but you must show cultural fit and enthusiasm
LLD is asked more than HLD for SDE-1 — practice class design

do: Show growth mindset — talk about what you learned
dont: Write pseudocode — they want real, runnable code
Skip dry-running your solution
Ignore the project discussion prep
Be arrogant — they value humility
Forget to ask clarifying questions

impressive_projects.title: Privacy-Focused Analytics SDK
impressive_projects.desc: Build an analytics tool that collects user behavior data while preserving differential privacy.

impressive_projects.title: High-Performance Image Processing
impressive_projects.desc: Real-time image filters and transformations using Metal/GPU acceleration.

impressive_projects.desc: Build a Spotify-like player with offline sync, queue management, and gapless playback.

must_have: iOS/macOS project is a strong signal

platforms.name: CoderPad
platforms.desc: Apple uses CoderPad for phone screens

interview_reviews.question: Merge Intervals + Trapping Rain Water
interview_reviews.quote: CoderPad 60-min, 2 problems. Clean, efficient code expected. They asked about memory management too.

interview_reviews.question: LRU Cache with thread-safety
interview_reviews.quote: Classic LRU but with mutex/lock consideration. Apple cares deeply about performance and concurrency.

interview_reviews.question: Design iCloud Photo Sync
interview_reviews.quote: They wanted Apple-specific thinking — battery optimization, offline-first, conflict resolution for synced photos.

interview_reviews.question: Why Apple? How do you handle disagreements?
interview_reviews.quote: They care about genuine passion for Apple products. Basic STAR format but be authentic.

insider_tips: Apple is extremely secretive — interviewers won't tell you the team until after offer
They value performance and optimization — mention Big-O in every solution
iOS/Swift knowledge is a major plus even for backend roles
System Design questions often involve Apple products — iCloud, iMessage, Apple Music
Less than 2% acceptance rate — be thoroughly prepared
They look for deep passion for their products — use Apple products and know them well

do: Show genuine passion for Apple products
dont: Apply without knowing Apple's product line
Ignore memory management
Write sloppy code — Apple values craft
Skip optimization discussions
Be generic about 'why Apple' — they can tell
